item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Okay, just making sure since some of them do and some of them don't. I wanted to make certain that I was upholding the license with my video. Thanks :)

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Hi AAN2,

SCP Wiki policy requires all images to be attributed. Think of it this way: we can't tell it's public domain if we can't verify it, right?

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
If it's public domain then why the addition at the bottom? As far as I'm aware, since it's public domain there isn't any special referencing anyone needs to do or licensing disclosures.
Am I missing something? Asking because I'm making a video about this skip lol

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image of an HS-125 aircraft could not be reliably sourced, the closest being here where it falls under copyright. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has been removed.

The second image originates from here, where it is in the public domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why it is not euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thoroughly enjoyed reading this, and the fact that it connects to a real world culture that is not in a westerner's general knowledge base makes the whole thing feel significantly more immersive.

I realize that this SCP is quite old, but I would like to see an update on it considering the significant damage done to the Yazidi people by the occupation of ISIL. It is my understanding that the people were more or less displaced and their numbers have been drastically reduced. I think it would require a fair amount of real-world research, but it could make for an intriguing update, if not a full-on tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, and it made me try and research the subject.

There is no reason to think this offensive. The SCP makes no assumptions about the Yazidi as people. It could easily be describing muslins, christians, buddhists or pastafarians when it comes to how it describes the actual people coming from the plane.

On the phenomenon, I found some few interesting things on the real-world Kiras Guhorîn. First, though I found no mention to some practice similar to this SCP, the Yazidi do have a couple holidays involving a pilgrimage, so it isn't an alien theme to them. Also, they believe that departed souls are judged in the land of the Peacock Angel, and the dead are buried turned to the East, so it seems logical that this land of judgement is on the East, the place from where the first plane is coming.

But the judgement is near to Heaven, and only the souls that achieved complete purification remain there. The others are sent back to reincarnate. So my interpretation is that the first plane arrive with the recently judged souls, with a crew of servants of heaven. The Yazidi are extremely closed, so it just makes sense that the responsibility for getting those souls to their place of preparation for reincarnation falls upon the holy men of their own people. The entities bringing those souls can have several reasons to not be able to complete the journey on their own. In the end, site 22B isn't quite a place on this world, and isn't theirs either.

And the Foundation isn't tasked with any of this, really. The Yazidi are, as they were before. At most the involved parties thrust the Foundation enough on their protection and discretion. If they have an actual choice to begging with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one superficially, for many reasons already listed, but would like to get the opinion of an actual Yazidi on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [Comment withdrawn.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Urgh, yeah. Some interesting ideas, but the fact that it's applied to a real-world ethnos makes me too uneasy. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Yazidi are not mythological or cryptozoological, they are an actual people.

Something worth keeping in mind.

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persecution_of_Yazidis_by_ISIL

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has aged noticeably what with the tone breaks and such but for some reason it still retains some charm to me. It feels totally plausible, and I'm always a fan of mythological/cryptozoological beings updating their modus operandi to keep with the times. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And we now know this is technically self-upvoted. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, thanks for clearing that up, then. Did I upvote this before?.. If not, I will do so now. The concept is interesting anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct. It's a non-issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it is the SCP Foundation. I've always thought it was up to author preference which one they used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is one thing that bothers me quite a bit: the constant use of SCP to refer to the Foundation and its assets, like in:

A passenger plane provided by SCP personnel (…)

and:

A fly over with an SCP reconnaissance plane (…)

I'm no senior staff, but I believe these and other instances should be written as "Foundation personnel", "Foundation reconnaissance plane", and so on.

I could be wrong, so I would appreciate some insight here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(they are) identified as various people of the Yazidi faith (through) specific knowledge questions

all have shown to be unable to recognize family and friends or been able to remember any information

Mutually exclusive paragraphs are mutually exclusive.

Also, I don't really like this one. Apart from the tone issues, which isn't a big problem for me, this one is confusing and uninteresting, and the Foundation behaves somewhat out of character. Also, the reincarnation part isn't explored at all, neither how it affects personal identity nor its connection to the rest of the Yazidi religion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with all of the previous posts… but I still up-voted it. It has such potential! Especially if the issues raised are addressed, namely the justification of having this middle-man airport and how the airport at the holy site came into existence (then again, the "airport" at the holy site could just be a bare strip of land)… I mean, what I pretty much assumed while reading this (though it would be better if this was explicit) is that the Yazidi trust the SCP enough to allow them knowledge of their holy pilgrimage site and provide a central hub for all the pilgrims to come to (though I think the pilgrims ought to come in multiple planes for the SCP owned airport to make sense), as the SCP would provide these oppressed people some protection (though why does the SCP need these Yazidi anyway? What did the Yazidi have that others couldn't provide? How did the Yazidi have the power to guarantee unrestricted airport access to the area?), and the Yazidi needed this airport because for some reason it would be more efficient (or impossible otherwise) if just one plane makes this weird supernatural voyage…

Also, I think some of the statements need to be rewritten for the sake of tone and clarity, such as "The crew are typically never able to elaborate upon details of the pilgrimage or what the kiras guhorîn actually is", which would imply that at least once the crew did in fact elaborate. If so, where is that information? And why are they not "able" to? Or do they not "want" to? Another picky thing… why would the information about how far the arrival plane appears on radar be important? What would be important is where the plane is coming from…

Also, it would be nice if the details about the SCP Mobile Task Force Sigma-4 mission to site-22B were more specific. I mean, details about what actually happened in the mission, I think, if fine to be left out or be expunged or whatever for creepy factor… but saying simply that the mission took place in the 1990's is way too vague and not in keeping with the ideals of the website and doesn't lend to verisimilitude, methinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one simply because it's just so unusual and relatively minor. It's the sort of thing I can very easily imagine happening unnoticed in some corner of the world.

Also, the Foundation aren't total sociopaths in this one, helping out disadvantaged religious minorities and all. I quite like that the Foundation hae a relationship with an unusual group that isn't 'lock them all up and dissect them'.

item.title: Re: Something I don't understand
item.content:encoded: Still, this is a hard one to wrap my head around.

item.title: Re: Something I don't understand
item.content:encoded: I believe the key here is the holy men. The pilgrims are brought to site 22A instead of directly to 22B because they have to be escorted by the holy men who now serve as the pilots of the plane the Foundation provides

item.title: Re: Something I don't understand
item.content:encoded:
Hm, from what it says it claims that SCP hot associated rights, which was the argued reason but yeah, if they can summon up a airport of their own, I imagine, getting a regulation accepted plane would be no problem. As to why SCP would have helped before hand is not really explained as well.

Yet…I shall upvote it.

item.title: Something I don't understand
item.content:encoded:
OK, so the Foundation steps in and gets an airfield, and all associated rights. Fine. Then, planes start arriving. Sure. And an airport mysteriously appears at the destination? Wait.

If there is one plane that has an unknown origin, and one SCP-supplied plane that takes all the same passengers that arrived hours earlier…why doesn't the first plane just go straight to Site-22B? It seems like it only stops at Site-22A so that the Foundation can conveniently study the travelers. If Site-22B can create an airport and tower that magically, why does the Foundation have anything to do with this at all?

I'm seriously missing something about this, I guess.

However, I do like it, maybe just because it makes me ask questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ridin' to reincarnation on a Segway? Oh my.

I like this SCP because of it's subtlety. There are no flesh-ripping [DATA EXPUNGED] lurking about the cabin, only a (relatively) mundane and quiet supernatural event. It works as a contrast to the SCPs that live only to rip your face off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, we have plenty of religious SCPs — but how many of them are non-Abrahamic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always liked this one.

Personally, I dig the idea of heavenly beings progressing technologically along with their terrestrial counterparts. I'd be willing to bet that back in the early days the pilgrimage was made by a fleet of chariots, then horse drawn carriages, then a bus, and may change into some new form as our transportation technology develops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, the writing is a bit muddled in areas, so it's sometimes hard to tell which 'flight' is the subject. Also, we have plenty of religious SCPs, but this one, I don't know….just can't put my finger on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still perplexed by this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's… eh… well, I think it would be better if…

Hrm…

It's one of those ones I can't put my finger on. Sorry, I'm usless here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that this is very well written, but it's slightly confusing and has logistics which are viscerally confusing.

Not to mention it brings in the whole religion dongle in a particularly odd way.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: I sort of agree with Lentil, but I would still like to keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I certainly wouldn't downvote it since it's written so well. But… I feel like I don't quite get this one.
